title: Chapter Twelve: The Doctor
content: He arrives at ten past eleven, a dark leather bag in one hand and a mild smile on his face. Dr. Richards. I watch from the upstairs landing as Mrs. Grose takes his coat — charcoal wool, expensive, the kind of coat that says *I charge by the hour and I'm worth it* — and leads him toward the drawing room. He moves through Bly like he's been here before. Like every manor house is the same house, and every anxious young woman is the same anxious young woman.
He's right, of course. That's the worst part.

I come down the stairs on schedule. No rushing, no hesitation. I've done this before — not as the subject, not for fifteen years, but I remember the choreography. Arrive on time. Sit where indicated. Keep your hands visible. Answer the questions in the order they're asked. Don't offer. Don't resist.

The drawing room has been reorganized. The settee where I usually sit has been pushed aside, replaced by two chairs facing each other across a low table. His bag sits on the table, open, a legal pad and a silver pen already resting on top. The brass clasp of the bag catches the gray morning light. The room smells like him already — cedar and something astringent, the ghost of a hospital corridor distilled into a single breath.

Mrs. Grose sets down a tray. Real tea. A porcelain pot, two cups, a small pitcher of milk. I watch her hands as she pours — steady, practiced, no tremor. She doesn't look at me. She sets the cup in front of me and withdraws without a word.
The door closes. The click of the latch is small and final.

"Ms. Bly." Dr. Richards extends his hand. His palm is warm and dry. His grip is firm but not testing. Everything about him says *I am not your enemy.* That's how the best ones operate. "Thank you for making time this morning."
"I wasn't given a choice," I say, then immediately regret it. Too blunt. Too honest. I need to be careful.
He doesn't flinch. He smiles — not condescending, not amused. Patient. "Mr. Wingrave asked me to come. He's concerned about you. I'm here to help."
"Where is Mr. Wingrave?"
"At the manor. Working, I believe. He thought it best to give us privacy." A pause. "This is a conversation, Ms. Bly. Not an interrogation."

I sit. He sits. The chairs are too close — five feet apart, maybe less. I can see the fine weave of his tie, the small stain on his left cuff where ink has bled into the fabric. He crosses one leg over the other and picks up the pen.
"Let's start with the basics. How have you been sleeping?"
"Fine."
"More than six hours a night?"
I hesitate. "Most nights."
"Dreams?"
"Everyone dreams."
"Nightmares?" His pen is poised. Waiting.
"Some."
He writes something. One word, maybe two. I can't read it from this angle. I don't try. I know what it says anyway. *Disturbed sleep.* It's the first checkbox on the form they don't show you.
"Appetite?"
"Adequate." The tea sits untouched between us. The steam has stopped rising.
"Have you experienced any visual disturbances? Seeing things that aren't there? Shapes, shadows, figures?"
The question lands like a hand on my shoulder in the dark. I keep my face still. "No."
"Any auditory disturbances? Hearing voices, sounds that can't be explained?"
"No."
"Any feelings of being watched? Followed? Persecuted?"

I look at him. He looks back. His face is open. Gentle. He smells like cedar and clinical concern, and I am seventeen again, sitting across from a woman in a white office with a clipboard, answering the same questions in the same order with the same hollow certainty that my answers don't matter, that the diagnosis was written before I walked in.
"No," I say.
He writes. The pen scratches against the legal pad. Scratch, scratch, scratch. A small sound. Meticulous.
"Let's talk about your history. Mr. Wingrave mentioned you spent some time in a hospital as a teenager. Is that correct?"
The air in the room tightens. "It is."
"Would you be willing to tell me about that?"
"It was a long time ago."
"I understand. But context matters. If you're comfortable—"

"I was seventeen." My voice comes out even. I've rehearsed this. Not for him — for everyone who would ever ask. "I experienced a period of psychological distress following a family trauma. I was hospitalized for observation and treatment. I was released after six weeks with no ongoing diagnosis. I've been fine since."
The pen moves. *Family trauma.* *Six weeks.* *No ongoing diagnosis.* He's writing down the facts, and between the facts he's writing the things he thinks I'm not saying.
"And this position at Bly — it's your first long-term placement since then?"
"Yes."
"What drew you to it?"
Here it comes. The trap. I feel it opening beneath me like a floor with a missing board.
"And you say you came to Bly specifically? You sought out this position?"
"Yes."
He tilts his head. Two degrees, maybe three. An angle designed to signal curiosity without judgment. "Why this position? There are families in London who need companions. Nannies. Tutors. Why here?"
The silence stretches. I can hear the clock in the hall, each tick a second I don't have an answer for.

Because Peter Quint was here. Because he died here. Because I spent three years becoming a psychologist to understand what happened to people like him, like me, and when I found his name in those records I couldn't let it go. Because something is wrong with these children and I need to know what it is.
Because I'm obsessed. Because I'm desperate. Because I came here with a secret and I'll leave with one too.
"The salary," I say. "And the children seemed lovely online."

The lie sits on the table between us like a third cup, empty and obvious. Dr. Richards regards me with the same gentle expression he's worn since he walked in. He's not fooled. His pen moves, and I imagine what he's writing: *Guarded. Evasive. Unconvincing rationale for placement.*

"Ms. Bly." He sets the pen down. Folds his hands. This is the part where kindness becomes a corridor with no exits. "I want to be straightforward with you. Based on what Mr. Wingrave has shared, and based on our conversation today, I'm going to recommend a period of observation and medication. A low-dose antipsychotic, just to stabilize."
The word *antipsychotic* enters the room and doesn't leave. It hangs in the air, dense and chemical, displacing oxygen.

"Just to stabilize," he repeats, as though the repetition makes it gentler. "There's no stigma. Many people in high-stress situations benefit from short-term pharmaceutical support. It's entirely voluntary, of course."
Of course. Voluntary. The same word they used at seventeen, right before they told me that voluntary was the alternative to involuntary, and wouldn't I prefer the first?
"I'll be following up weekly," he says. "Mr. Wingrave has generously agreed to fund the treatment."

There it is. Henry's hand, extended from a distance — not a hand of comfort but of transaction. He pays the doctor. He pays me. He pays for Bly, for the children, for the walls that hold this whole arrangement upright. And now he pays for my medication, the way he might pay for a leaking pipe or a broken latch. Something to be fixed. Something that makes noise and shouldn't.

Dr. Richards reaches into his bag and produces a small orange bottle. He sets it on the table next to the tea I haven't touched. The bottle is warm from the interior of the bag. The white plastic cap has a child-proof ridged top.
"I recommend starting tonight. One before bed. It may take a few days to find the right balance, but you should notice a difference within a week."

A difference. A flattening. A fog that rolls in at the edges of every thought and muffles the world until you can't remember why you cared about anything. I know the difference. I've lived the difference. The person I was at seventeen — intensity and clarity and a mind that burned too bright — she was a problem to be solved. They solved her. They smoothed her edges and dimmed her light and sent her back into the world paler and quieter and *manageable*.
"You'll feel much better," Dr. Richards says.

He believes it. That's the worst thing. He's not cruel. He's not indifferent. He believes, with the quiet conviction of every professional who's ever looked at a burning house and decided the solution was less fire, that he is helping. That the fog is peace. That the flatness is stability.
He stands. I stand. We shake hands again. His palm is still warm.
"Take care of yourself, Ms. Bly."

The door opens. Mrs. Grose is there, waiting to see him out. He says something to her in a low voice and she nods. They move toward the front hall. The front door opens, closes. Tire crunch on gravel. Then nothing.
I stand in the drawing room with the orange bottle on the table and the cold tea beside it. The room is empty. The house is quiet. I pick up the bottle.

It weighs almost nothing. The pills rattle inside — small, white, identical. I turn the bottle over. The label has my name on it. *Maeve Bly.* Below that, the dosage. Below that, the prescriber's name in neat small print. Everything in order. Everything official. A paper trail that says I am the problem at Bly.
I climb the stairs to my room. Each step is deliberate. I close the door. Sit on the edge of the bed. The mattress dips beneath me, familiar and unwanted.

I unscrew the cap. The pills are small and round and white, like tiny eyes staring up from the orange plastic. I could take one right now. Just one. Just to stabilize. Just to feel the fog roll in and the edges soften and the burning house go quiet.

I've been here before. I was seventeen. I sat on a bed that wasn't mine in a room that wasn't mine and I held a bottle that looked exactly like this one and I thought: *This is what getting better looks like.* It looked like forgetting. It looked like agreeing. It looked like becoming the kind of person who sits in a drawing room and says *fine* and *adequate* and *no* while someone writes down the truth she won't say.
I set the bottle on the nightstand. The pills stay inside.
I won't take them.

But I don't flush them either. I sit very still and I think about the tea sample in my suitcase, sealed in its plastic bag, labeled in my handwriting. Evidence. And now this — a prescription bottle with my name, prescribed by a doctor I didn't request, funded by a man who won't sit in the same room with me. More evidence.

I open the suitcase. The tea sample is there, next to my folded clothes, next to the notebook where I've written everything I've seen. I place the orange bottle beside it. The plastic crinkles as it settles against the lining. Prescription and poison, side by side. A matched set.

Someone is trying to control what I see, what I think, what I feel. The tea did it from the inside — numbing the edges, blunting the vision. Now the pills will do it from the outside, sanctioned and salaried and signed by my employer. Different methods, same goal. Don't see. Don't question. Don't burn.

They've been doing this from the start. The tea. The gaslighting. The children's performances. Henry's distance dressed as concern. And now this — the final touch, the medical seal on the diagnosis that says *she's not reliable, she's not well, don't listen to anything she tells you.* It's elegant, in its way. By the time I try to speak, no one will believe a word.
But they've made one mistake.
They think I'll take the pills.

That night, I lie in the dark and stare at the ceiling. The curtains are open. Moonlight falls across the bed in a pale diagonal. The house is silent — no footsteps, no whispers, no sounds from the children's wing. Even Bly is holding its breath.
I don't take the pill. I don't take the tea. I don't do anything except lie here, stone-cold sober and stone-cold calm, and for the first time since I arrived at this house, I am not afraid.

Not of the children. Not of the house. Not of what I've seen in the shadows of the east wing or heard in the pause between the grandfather clock's chimes. Not of Henry and his checkbook, not of Mrs. Grose and her silence, not of the doctor and his gentle, killing kindness.
I'm not even angry anymore. Anger is hot and loud and obvious. Anger gets you prescribed, observed, stabilized. Anger is what they're expecting.
What I am is clear. The clarity is worse than anger. It's worse than fear. It's a blade instead of a fire — no flame to smother, no heat to medicate, just a clean edge that cuts without smoke.

I know what I am to them. I'm the unreliable narrator of my own life. The girl who was hospitalized at seventeen, who sought out a position in a house where a man died, who claims to see things that can't be explained. The diagnosis writes itself. I've read the literature. I've *written* the literature. I know exactly how this story goes — the concerned employer, the well-meaning doctor, the medication that makes everything manageable, the quiet departure, the confidential file.
But I also know what they don't.

I know about the tea. I know about Miles's smile when Henry took his side — the checkmate smile of a child who's played this game before. I know that Flora's sweetness is a performance as choreographed as any line in a script. I know that Peter Quint died in this house and that no one will talk about it, and I know that the silence around his death is not grief but architecture — something built, something maintained, something that requires effort.

They think they've seen me. The anxious one. The fragile one. The one who can be managed with a prescription and a firm hand. They've read my file — the hospitalization, the psychology degree, the thin line between professional interest and personal obsession. They think they know how this ends.
They haven't seen anything yet.
I whisper it to the ceiling, to the dark, to whatever is listening in the walls of Bly Manor.
"You think you've seen me."
The moon shifts. The light on the bed moves an inch.
"You haven't seen anything yet."
And I close my eyes, and I sleep — not the drugged sleep of the tea, not the fitful sleep of fear, but the clean, dark sleep of someone who has finally stopped running and started planning.
